frames.caption: Stream the numbers 5, 2, 8, 1. Keep the smaller half in a MAX-heap (low) and the larger half in a MIN-heap (high) so both middles sit at a root.
frames.note: A single sorted list would make insertion O(n). Two heaps split the work: each add is O(log n) and the median is then just a peek at one or both roots — O(1). The roots are the only elements we ever need to read for the median, so the heaps' internal order beyond the root is irrelevant.

frames.caption: Add 5: push it into low (max-heap). low = {5}.
frames.note: Every add starts the same way: push the new number into `low`, the max-heap of the smaller half. We do not yet know which half it belongs to, so we drop it in low and let the funnel sort it out. A max-heap keeps its LARGEST element at the root in O(log n), which is exactly the boundary value we may need to hand off.

frames.caption: Move low's max (5) into high (min-heap). low = {∅}, high = {5}.
frames.note: Move low's current maximum into `high`, the min-heap of the larger half. This single hand-off is what guarantees the cross-heap invariant: after it, the largest of the values that passed through low has been pushed toward the upper half, so every element left in low ends up <= every element in high. high keeps its SMALLEST at the root, the other median candidate.

frames.caption: high (1) outgrew low (0): move high's min (5) back to low. low = {5}, high = {∅}.
frames.note: Rebalance for size: if high now holds more than low, pop high's minimum and push it back into low. We only ever let low be equal to high or larger by one — never smaller. Keeping low the (weakly) bigger heap is what makes FindMedian branchless: odd total => the lone middle is low's root.

frames.caption: Odd total (1): median = low.Peek() = 5.
frames.note: Odd count: low has one more element than high, so the single middle value IS low's root — read it in O(1). All the O(log n) work happened during add; FindMedian just peeks. That O(log n) add / O(1) read split is the whole reason to pay for two heaps instead of re-sorting the stream each query.

frames.caption: Add 2: push it into low (max-heap). low = {5, 2}.
frames.note: Every add starts the same way: push the new number into `low`, the max-heap of the smaller half. We do not yet know which half it belongs to, so we drop it in low and let the funnel sort it out. A max-heap keeps its LARGEST element at the root in O(log n), which is exactly the boundary value we may need to hand off.

frames.caption: Move low's max (5) into high (min-heap). low = {2}, high = {5}.
frames.note: Move low's current maximum into `high`, the min-heap of the larger half. This single hand-off is what guarantees the cross-heap invariant: after it, the largest of the values that passed through low has been pushed toward the upper half, so every element left in low ends up <= every element in high. high keeps its SMALLEST at the root, the other median candidate.

frames.caption: Sizes 1,1: high did not outgrow low, so no rebalance. Invariant holds: every low ≤ every high.
frames.note: high did not outgrow low, so no rebalance is needed. The sizes already differ by at most one with low >= high, the invariant holds, and the two roots straddle the middle of the sorted stream.

frames.caption: Even total (2): median = (low.Peek() + high.Peek()) / 2 = (2 + 5) / 2 = 3.5.
frames.note: Even count: the two middles are low's root (largest of the smaller half) and high's root (smallest of the larger half). Because the invariant guarantees low.root <= high.root, their average is the true median — computed in O(1) with no scan, no sort.

frames.caption: Add 8: push it into low (max-heap). low = {8, 2}.
frames.note: Every add starts the same way: push the new number into `low`, the max-heap of the smaller half. We do not yet know which half it belongs to, so we drop it in low and let the funnel sort it out. A max-heap keeps its LARGEST element at the root in O(log n), which is exactly the boundary value we may need to hand off.

frames.caption: Move low's max (8) into high (min-heap). low = {2}, high = {5, 8}.
frames.note: Move low's current maximum into `high`, the min-heap of the larger half. This single hand-off is what guarantees the cross-heap invariant: after it, the largest of the values that passed through low has been pushed toward the upper half, so every element left in low ends up <= every element in high. high keeps its SMALLEST at the root, the other median candidate.

frames.caption: high (2) outgrew low (1): move high's min (5) back to low. low = {5, 2}, high = {8}.
frames.note: Rebalance for size: if high now holds more than low, pop high's minimum and push it back into low. We only ever let low be equal to high or larger by one — never smaller. Keeping low the (weakly) bigger heap is what makes FindMedian branchless: odd total => the lone middle is low's root.

frames.caption: Odd total (3): median = low.Peek() = 5.
frames.note: Odd count: low has one more element than high, so the single middle value IS low's root — read it in O(1). All the O(log n) work happened during add; FindMedian just peeks. That O(log n) add / O(1) read split is the whole reason to pay for two heaps instead of re-sorting the stream each query.

frames.caption: Add 1: push it into low (max-heap). low = {5, 2, 1}.
frames.note: Every add starts the same way: push the new number into `low`, the max-heap of the smaller half. We do not yet know which half it belongs to, so we drop it in low and let the funnel sort it out. A max-heap keeps its LARGEST element at the root in O(log n), which is exactly the boundary value we may need to hand off.

frames.caption: Move low's max (5) into high (min-heap). low = {2, 1}, high = {5, 8}.
frames.note: Move low's current maximum into `high`, the min-heap of the larger half. This single hand-off is what guarantees the cross-heap invariant: after it, the largest of the values that passed through low has been pushed toward the upper half, so every element left in low ends up <= every element in high. high keeps its SMALLEST at the root, the other median candidate.

frames.caption: Sizes 2,2: high did not outgrow low, so no rebalance. Invariant holds: every low ≤ every high.
frames.note: high did not outgrow low, so no rebalance is needed. The sizes already differ by at most one with low >= high, the invariant holds, and the two roots straddle the middle of the sorted stream.

frames.caption: Even total (4): median = (low.Peek() + high.Peek()) / 2 = (2 + 5) / 2 = 3.5.
frames.note: Even count: the two middles are low's root (largest of the smaller half) and high's root (smallest of the larger half). Because the invariant guarantees low.root <= high.root, their average is the true median — computed in O(1) with no scan, no sort.

frames.caption: Stream done. low = {2, 1} (max-root 2), high = {5, 8} (min-root 5); every low ≤ every high, so median = (2+5)/2 = 3.5.
frames.note: The two roots, 2 and 5, straddle the middle of the sorted stream 1, 2, 5, 8 — their average 3.5 is the median. Each add cost O(log n) for the heap ops; every FindMedian was a single O(1) peek. That is the running-median pattern: whenever a problem repeatedly asks for "the middle as data arrives," reach for a max-heap of the low half and a min-heap of the high half.
